_**Agent (claude-opus-4-7 2026-04-29 20:41:18Z)**_

Both questions answered:

**1. Is there enough info in `breakpoint_files/` to recover nearby-cluster sequences?**

Yes. The `.nearby.sam` files have full SAM rows (column 10 = the read sequence). I confirmed: read `a23e1cd3-…` has a 13,216-base sequence and matching qual string in `chr15-74024222-chr17-40346374.sam`. With those plus `.nearby.readnamescoords` (read → encoded breakpoint per read) you have everything `breakpointPileup.pl` needs on the **read** side. And [/mnt/pikachu/bff-test/bps](/mnt/pikachu/bff-test/bps) is already the breakpoint file in the right format.

One small wrinkle: those `.sam` files are alignment lines only, no `@HD`/`@SQ` header. To run mpileup you'd need to prepend a header (re-emit one from a sorted+indexed source BAM, or generate it from the FASTA's `.fai`) before sorting and indexing.

**2. Can we use `hg38-primary.mmi` instead of a FASTA?**

No. `.mmi` is a minimap2 *minimizer index* — a hashed/sketched lookup for alignment. It does not store the reference sequence in retrievable form. You can't run `samtools faidx <chr:pos-pos>` against it, and `samtools mpileup -f` won't accept it. mpileup needs a real FASTA + `.fai` to pull the reference base at every column.

So to test in the wild you need:
- the hg38 primary FASTA that was used to build that `.mmi` (whatever `minimap2 -d hg38-primary.mmi …` was given as input — typically `GRCh38_primary.fa` or similar). Index it with `samtools faidx`.
- a sorted+indexed BAM containing the supporting reads — easiest is to derive it from `bam/final.sam` (which presumably already has the header), since it should already contain the reads named in `breakpoint_files/readnames`.

Want me to sketch a one-shot prep script that takes a FASTA path + the existing `bam/final.sam`, builds the indexed BAM, and runs `breakpointPileup.pl` against `bps`?

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-7 2026-04-29 20:47:58Z)**_

Two real wrinkles surfaced from inspecting the data:

- **Naming mismatch.** The cellranger reference uses Ensembl-style contig names (`1`, `2`, …, `X`, `Y`, `MT`); the SAM files use UCSC-style (`chr15`, `chr17`). mpileup needs them aligned. The wrapper has to either rename the FASTA (3.1GB rewrite — wasteful) or strip `chr` on the SAM/breakpoint side. I'll do the strip-on-the-fly approach since it's free.
- **No `.fai`** next to the FASTA. Wrapper will build it lazily, but only if the directory is writable.

Apart from that the existing `biodepot/bff:test` image already has every dependency (perl + File::Temp from core, samtools, breakpointPileup.pl). I'll add the wrapper to the same image rather than spinning a new one up.

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-7 2026-04-29 20:48:24Z)**_

<tool-use data-tool-type="write" data-tool-name="Write"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Write**</summary>

```sh
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# processBreakpointDir.sh
#
# Build fusion consensus sequences for every breakpoint cluster in a
# directory whose layout matches the BFF "breakpoint_files/" output —
# pairs of {name}.nearby.sam (alignment dump, no @HD/@SQ) plus
# {name}.nearby.readnamescoords (TSV: read_name<TAB>chr:s:e;chr:s:e).
#
# For each cluster we:
#   1. Prepend a header derived from the reference's .fai so samtools
#      can sort the SAM into a coordinate-sorted, indexed BAM.
#   2. Derive the reads list from column 1 of the .readnamescoords.
#   3. Pick the modal encoding from column 2 as the breakpoint file row
#      (matches the canonical breakpoint of the cluster).
#   4. Run breakpointPileup.pl, writing {name}.fa (consensus) and
#      {name}.tsv (per-position counts) into the output directory.
#
# If the SAM uses "chr"-prefixed contig names but the reference does not
# (e.g. cellranger's Ensembl-style FASTA), we strip the "chr" prefix on
# the fly from both the SAM RNAME/RNEXT and the breakpoint encoding.
#
# Usage:
#   processBreakpointDir.sh INPUT_DIR REFERENCE_FASTA OUTPUT_DIR [WINDOW]
#
# Requires samtools, perl, and breakpointPileup.pl on PATH.

set -euo pipefail

inDir=${1:-}
ref=${2:-}
outDir=${3:-}
window=${4:-100}

if [[ -z "$inDir" || -z "$ref" || -z "$outDir" ]]; then
    echo "usage: $0 INPUT_DIR REFERENCE_FASTA OUTPUT_DIR [WINDOW]" >&2
    exit 1
fi

[[ -d "$inDir" ]] || { echo "input dir not found: $inDir" >&2; exit 1; }
[[ -f "$ref"  ]]  || { echo "reference not found: $ref"   >&2; exit 1; }

if [[ ! -f "$ref.fai" ]]; then
    echo "indexing $ref (one-time)..." >&2
    samtools faidx "$ref"
fi

mkdir -p "$outDir"

# --- decide whether SAM's "chr"-style names need stripping --------------------

faiHasChr=0
firstFaiName=$(awk 'NR==1{print $1; exit}' "$ref.fai")
[[ "$firstFaiName" == chr* ]] && faiHasChr=1

# --- header (one @SQ per FAI contig, no @PG) ---------------------------------

header=$(mktemp)
trap 'rm -f "$header"' EXIT
{
    printf '@HD\tVN:1.6\tSO:coordinate\n'
    awk -v OFS='\t' '{print "@SQ", "SN:" $1, "LN:" $2}' "$ref.fai"
} >"$header"

# --- per-cluster work -------------------------------------------------------

shopt -s nullglob
processed=0
skipped=0
for samFile in "$inDir"/*.nearby.sam; do
    base=$(basename "$samFile" .nearby.sam)
    coordsFile="$inDir/${base}.nearby.readnamescoords"
    if [[ ! -f "$coordsFile" ]]; then
        echo "warn: missing $coordsFile, skipping $base" >&2
        skipped=$((skipped+1))
        continue
    fi

    # Decide whether this cluster needs chr-strip (per-cluster in case the
    # input dir mixes naming, though normally it won't).
    samHasChr=0
    samFirstRname=$(awk -F'\t' '!/^@/{print $3; exit}' "$samFile")
    [[ "$samFirstRname" == chr* ]] && samHasChr=1
    stripChr=0
    if [[ $samHasChr -eq 1 && $faiHasChr -eq 0 ]]; then stripChr=1; fi

    work=$(mktemp -d)

    # 1. header + (optionally renamed) records -> sorted+indexed BAM
    if [[ $stripChr -eq 1 ]]; then
        {
            cat "$header"
            awk -v FS='\t' -v OFS='\t' \
                '{ sub(/^chr/, "", $3); if ($7 != "*" && $7 != "=") sub(/^chr/, "", $7); print }' \
                "$samFile"
        }
    else
        cat "$header" "$samFile"
    fi | samtools sort -O bam -o "$work/cluster.bam" -
    samtools index "$work/cluster.bam"

    # 2. reads list
    cut -f1 "$coordsFile" | sort -u >"$work/reads.txt"

    # 3. breakpoint file: modal encoding across rows, optionally chr-stripped
    if [[ $stripChr -eq 1 ]]; then
        cut -f2 "$coordsFile" | sed 's/\(^\|;\)chr/\1/g' \
            | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn | head -1 | awk '{print $2}' >"$work/bp.txt"
    else
        cut -f2 "$coordsFile" \
            | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn | head -1 | awk '{print $2}' >"$work/bp.txt"
    fi

    if [[ ! -s "$work/bp.txt" ]]; then
        echo "warn: no breakpoint encoding in $coordsFile, skipping $base" >&2
        rm -rf "$work"
        skipped=$((skipped+1))
        continue
    fi

    # 4. consensus + variant table
    breakpointPileup.pl \
        --reads     "$work/reads.txt" \
        --bam       "$work/cluster.bam" \
        --ref       "$ref" \
        -b          "$work/bp.txt" \
        --window    "$window" \
        --pileupOut "$outDir/${base}.tsv" \
        >"$outDir/${base}.fa"

    rm -rf "$work"
    echo "ok: $base -> $outDir/${base}.fa, $outDir/${base}.tsv"
    processed=$((processed+1))
done

echo "processed=$processed skipped=$skipped output=$outDir"

```

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-7 2026-04-29 20:50:10Z)**_

Both rows have count 1 → the "modal" tie-breaker silently picked row 2 by lex order. Tighter approach: when the filename gives a canonical bp pair, prefer the row that matches it exactly; fall back to modal only when no row matches. That makes results stable across re-runs.

What I did:
- New [bin/processBreakpointDir.sh](bin/processBreakpointDir.sh) wrapper. Handles the SAM-without-header (synthesizes `@SQ` from the .fai), the chr-prefix mismatch (auto-detects and strips), and the cluster-encoding pick (filename-canonical first, modal as fallback).
- Same script copied to [widgets/.../Dockerfiles/processBreakpointDir.sh](widgets/fast_fusion_finder/BiodepotFusionFinder/Dockerfiles/processBreakpointDir.sh), `ADD`ed in the Dockerfile, image rebuilt as `biodepot/bff:test`.
- Verified end-to-end against your real data on the cellranger Ensembl FASTA.

One-time: I had to `samtools faidx` the cellranger FASTA so mpileup can use it; the .fai now sits next to it. The script will faidx automatically if missing, but only if the directory is writable.

Add processBreakpointDir.sh for batch consensus over a breakpoint dir

bin/processBreakpointDir.sh
- Iterates each cluster in a BFF "breakpoint_files/"-style directory and
  runs breakpointPileup.pl per cluster, writing {name}.fa (consensus) and
  {name}.tsv (per-position counts) into an output dir.
- Synthesizes a SAM @HD/@SQ header from the reference's .fai so the
  cluster's headerless .nearby.sam can be sorted+indexed.
- Auto-detects UCSC vs Ensembl contig naming: if the SAM uses chr-prefixed
  names but the FAI doesn't, strips chr from RNAME, RNEXT, and the
  breakpoint-file encoding so mpileup can find the reference.
- Picks the breakpoint-file row whose (end-of-side-1, start-of-side-2)
  matches the filename's canonical chrA-posA-chrB-posB pair; falls back
  to the modal encoding when no row matches. Avoids the lex-order tie
  break that picked an off-by-one bp on small clusters.
- Faidx's the FASTA lazily on first run if no .fai is present.

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-7 2026-04-29 21:03:28Z)**_

<tool-use data-tool-type="write" data-tool-name="Write"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Write**</summary>

```md
# Fusion breakpoint consensus

Build per-base consensus sequences over the supporting reads for every
fusion breakpoint detected by the BFF pipeline. The two halves of each
fusion are emitted as **separate FASTA records** (5' partner, then 3'
partner), windowed around the junction; columns where a non-reference
base wins a strict majority of the reads are emitted in lowercase.

Two entry points:

- [`bin/breakpointPileup.pl`](bin/breakpointPileup.pl) — the core script.
  Consumes a read-names list, the original BAM, the reference FASTA, and
  a breakpoint file; produces one FASTA record per side and (optionally)
  a per-position TSV of base counts. Use this when you already have
  those four pieces.
- [`bin/processBreakpointDir.sh`](bin/processBreakpointDir.sh) — batch
  wrapper for a `breakpoint_files/`-style directory. For each cluster it
  rebuilds the SAM into a sorted BAM, derives the reads list and the
  breakpoint encoding from `*.nearby.readnamescoords`, and runs the core
  script. Use this when you have BFF cluster output and a reference.

## Layout

\```
bin/
├── breakpointPileup.pl       canonical script
├── processBreakpointDir.sh   batch wrapper
├── filterEnds.pl             upstream: read-name filter
└── ...
breakpointPileup.pl           symlink → bin/breakpointPileup.pl
tests/test_breakpointPileup.sh   self-contained fixture test
widgets/.../Dockerfiles/      copies that get baked into biodepot/bff:test
\```

`breakpointPileup.pl` lives at `bin/breakpointPileup.pl`; the top-level
file is a symlink for convenience. The Dockerfiles directory holds a
build-context copy of each script (kept in sync manually).

## Requirements

There is nothing to compile.

- `samtools` 1.12 or newer on `$PATH` (1.12 added `samtools view -N`,
  the read-name filter the script depends on)
- `perl` 5 with `Getopt::Long` and `File::Temp` (both core)
- the reference FASTA must have a `.fai` (built lazily by the wrapper
  when missing, but only if the directory is writable)

Verify:

\```bash
samtools --version | head -1   # 1.12+
perl -c bin/breakpointPileup.pl
\```

## Three ways to run

### 1. Container (zero host setup)

The `biodepot/bff:test` image bundles `samtools`, `perl`, and both
scripts under `/usr/local/bin`.

\```bash
# build once
docker build -t biodepot/bff:test \
    widgets/fast_fusion_finder/BiodepotFusionFinder/Dockerfiles

# run on a breakpoint_files-style directory
docker run --rm \
    -u "$(id -u):$(id -g)" \
    -v /path/to/breakpoint_files:/in:ro \
    -v /path/to/reference_dir:/ref:ro \
    -v /path/to/output:/out \
    biodepot/bff:test \
    processBreakpointDir.sh /in /ref/genome.fa /out 100
\```

The `-u` flag makes the output files owned by your host user instead of
root. The reference directory needs the `.fa.fai`; create it once on
the host with `samtools faidx genome.fa` if it isn't there.

### 2. Standalone

If `samtools` and `perl` are on the host, just put the repo's `bin/`
on `$PATH` (so the wrapper can find `breakpointPileup.pl`) and run:

\```bash
PATH="$PWD/bin:$PATH" \
bin/processBreakpointDir.sh \
    /path/to/breakpoint_files \
    /path/to/genome.fa \
    /path/to/output \
    100
\```

### 3. As part of the BFF widget pipeline

`runBFF.sh` (the widget entrypoint) calls `breakpointPileup.pl`
automatically when both `$BAM` and `$REFERENCE` are set:

\```bash
WINDOW=100 \
pileup=/data/fusion.fa \
pileupTable=/data/variants.tsv \
filterends=/data/reads.txt \
BAM=/data/all.sorted.bam REFERENCE=/data/genome.fa \
runBFF.sh --first first.bam --last last.bam -b breakpoints.txt
\```

If `$BAM` or `$REFERENCE` is empty the pileup step is skipped and
`runBFF.sh` runs `filterEnds.pl` alone, matching the legacy behaviour.

## Inputs

### For `processBreakpointDir.sh`

| Path | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `INPUT_DIR` | A `breakpoint_files/`-style directory with `*.nearby.sam` (alignment dump, no `@HD`/`@SQ`) and matching `*.nearby.readnamescoords`. |
| `REFERENCE_FASTA` | The genome FASTA used for alignment. Needs a `.fai`. |
| `OUTPUT_DIR` | Created if missing. One `<name>.fa` and one `<name>.tsv` per cluster. |
| `WINDOW` | Optional half-window size in bp (default `100`). Each side gets `WINDOW` bases on the transcript-internal flank of the breakpoint. |

The wrapper auto-detects UCSC vs Ensembl contig naming. If the SAM uses
`chr15`-style names but the reference doesn't, `chr` is stripped on the
fly from `RNAME`, `RNEXT`, and the breakpoint encoding before mpileup.

### For `breakpointPileup.pl`

| Flag | Required | Notes |
|------|----------|-------|
| `--reads reads.txt` | yes | Newline-separated read names — typically the stdout of `filterEnds.pl`. |
| `--bam in.bam` | yes | Coordinate-sorted and indexed. |
| `--ref ref.fa` | yes | With matching `.fai`. |
| `-b breakpoints.txt` | yes | One fusion per line: `chr:start:end;chr:start:end`. Strand encoded by coord order — `start<end` is `+`, `start>end` is `-`. Lines starting with `#` are ignored. |
| `--window N` | no, default 100 | bp per side. |
| `--pileupOut path` | no | Write a per-position TSV (chr, pos, ref, consensus, depth, A, C, G, T) alongside stdout. |
| `--threshold F` | no, default 0.5 | Strict majority needed to call a non-reference base. |
| `--minDepth D` | no, default 1 | Columns below this depth fall back to the reference. |
| `--guideFile guides.txt` (`-G`) | no | `group<TAB>chr:pos<TAB>+/-`. Overrides the breakpoint-file's encoded strand and groups output by guide name. |

## Output

For each non-comment line of the breakpoint file, two FASTA records
are written to stdout (or to a file if you redirect):

\```
>15:74024123-74024222+ length=100
GAGAGTCTCCAGGAGTCTTTGACTAAATCTCCAAGCTGGAATGTGAGCTCTGAGCAGCTAGTATAGGATGCCCTTACTGG
GAAAGGGGAGGGAGGCTATG
>17:40346374-40346473+ length=100
GAGAGGCGGGGCCCAGGGCAAACGGTGGATTAGGAGGGGTGGGGAGGTCAGTGCCTTCTTCCTCTGCTTGTCGGAATGCT
GACCAAGATTCTAGGCCATG
\```

Headers are `<region><strand> length=<N>`; sides are emitted 5' first
then 3', in the order they appear on the breakpoint line.
Lowercase letters mark columns where a non-reference base won
`> --threshold` of the parsed reads. The strand annotation in the
header reflects what was decoded from the breakpoint file's coord
order (or, with `--guideFile`, the guide direction).

When `--pileupOut` is set, the companion TSV has one row per pileup
column **in genomic order** (so for `-` strand sides the row order
is the reverse of the FASTA letter order):

\```
#chr   pos    ref  consensus  depth  A  C  G  T
15     74024124  G  G   2  0  0  2  0
15     74024125  A  a   8  0  0  6  2
...
\```

## Conventions worth knowing

- **Strand from coord order.** The upstream BFF generator emits each
  side with `start<end` for `+` and `start>end` for `-`. The script
  trusts that encoding — no fallback to BAM FLAGs. If you supply your
  own breakpoint file, follow the same convention or use `--guideFile`.
- **Breakpoint position per side.** Side 1 (5' partner) → second coord
  of the encoded `chr:start:end`. Side 2 (3' partner) → first coord.
  This matches what `findBreakPoints` in `filterEnds.pl` writes.
- **Window placement.** `5'+` and `3'-` use `[bp-W+1, bp]`; `5'-` and
  `3'+` use `[bp, bp+W-1]`. So each FASTA record is `W` bases of the
  partner gene's transcript-side flank ending at (or starting at) the
  junction.
- **Variant case.** Lowercase = non-reference call. Reverse complement
  preserves case (`tr/ACGTNacgtn/TGCANtgcan/`).

## Smoke test

`tests/test_breakpointPileup.sh` builds a synthetic chr1/chr2 reference
and ten reads, then exercises forward+forward, narrow window,
forward+reverse with RC variants, and zero-coverage fallback:

\```bash
bash tests/test_breakpointPileup.sh
\```

All assertions should pass. The same suite runs unmodified inside the
container against `/usr/local/bin/breakpointPileup.pl`.

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-7 2026-04-29 21:06:27Z)**_

<tool-use data-tool-type="write" data-tool-name="Write"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Write**</summary>

```sh
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# extractReadsFasta.sh — pull reads by name from a FASTQ and emit them as FASTA.
#
# Usage:
#   extractReadsFasta.sh NAMES_FILE FASTQ [FASTQ ...]  > out.fa
#
# - NAMES_FILE is a text file of read names. Only the first whitespace-
#   separated field of each line is consulted, so a TSV (e.g. the
#   .readnamescoords used elsewhere in this repo: "name<TAB>chr:s:e;...")
#   works without preprocessing. Lines starting with '#' are ignored.
# - FASTQ may be plain or gzipped (auto-detected by the '.gz' suffix).
# - Multiple FASTQs can be passed; they're concatenated in the order
#   given. Output records appear in FASTQ order (not names-file order).
# - Names not found in any FASTQ are listed on stderr at the end.
# - Read-name comparison strips the leading '@' and any trailing
#   whitespace+comment from the FASTQ header line, so headers like
#   "@abc123 length=12345 ..." match the bare name "abc123".
#
# Portability: works with gawk or mawk, requires only POSIX awk
# features. No Perl, no Python, no samtools. zcat is needed only for
# gzipped inputs.

set -euo pipefail

if [[ $# -lt 2 ]]; then
    echo "usage: $0 NAMES_FILE FASTQ [FASTQ ...] > out.fa" >&2
    exit 1
fi

names=$1
shift

[[ -f "$names" ]] || { echo "names file not found: $names" >&2; exit 1; }

# Concatenate every FASTQ (decompressing .gz on the fly) into one stream
# and let awk pluck the wanted records out of it.
{
    for f in "$@"; do
        [[ -f "$f" ]] || { echo "fastq not found: $f" >&2; exit 1; }
        case "$f" in
            *.gz)  zcat -- "$f";;
            *)     cat  -- "$f";;
        esac
    done
} | awk -v namesFile="$names" '
BEGIN {
    nWanted = 0
    while ((getline line < namesFile) > 0) {
        sub(/^[[:space:]]+/, "", line)
        if (line == "" || substr(line, 1, 1) == "#") continue
        n = split(line, parts, /[[:space:]]+/)
        if (parts[1] != "" && !(parts[1] in wanted)) {
            wanted[parts[1]] = 1
            nWanted++
        }
    }
    close(namesFile)
    if (nWanted == 0) {
        print "no read names parsed from " namesFile > "/dev/stderr"
        exit 1
    }
}

# Walk the concatenated FASTQ four lines at a time. Sequence lines never
# start with "@" by spec, but quality lines can; reading exactly four
# lines from a known "@" header keeps us in sync.
{
    if (substr($0, 1, 1) != "@") next
    name = substr($0, 2)
    sub(/[[:space:]].*$/, "", name)
    if ((getline seq)  <= 0) exit
    if ((getline plus) <= 0) exit
    if ((getline qual) <= 0) exit
    if (name in wanted && !(name in seen)) {
        print ">" name
        print seq
        seen[name] = 1
        nFound++
    }
}

END {
    if (nFound + 0 < nWanted) {
        for (n in wanted) {
            if (!(n in seen)) print "missing: " n > "/dev/stderr"
        }
        printf("extracted %d/%d reads\n", nFound + 0, nWanted) > "/dev/stderr"
    }
}
'

```

I'd like plan a different variant of the breakpoint finder that aligns the reads using the help of the reference. It requires split aligning the reads by maximizing their alignment scores to both sides of the reference. Then take the consensus sequence of the spanning region across the break using the alignment as the matching frame. How would I do this?

---

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-7 2026-04-30 03:57:10Z)**_

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-7 2026-04-30 03:57:21Z)**_

The cleanest way is to flip the problem on its head: instead of running mpileup against the genome twice and stitching the consensus, **build a chimeric reference per fusion (W bp of 5' partner + W bp of 3' partner, in transcript order) and realign the supporting reads to it with minimap2**. The realignment is a normal split-into-one-contig alignment, so mpileup against the chimeric ref gives you a single contiguous pileup across the junction — bases that were soft-clipped in the genome alignment now align properly, indels right at the breakpoint are explicit, and the consensus comes out as one record naturally.

Concretely:

1. For each fusion, take the existing per-side consensus FASTA we already build (or just the reference windows) and concatenate them into `>fusion_X` — RC sides where the breakpoint encodes `-`. That's your chimeric reference.
2. Pull the supporting reads with [extractReadsFasta.sh](bin/extractReadsFasta.sh) (we just wrote this) into one FASTQ.
3. `minimap2 -ax map-ont chimeric.fa reads.fq | samtools sort -o realigned.bam && samtools index realigned.bam`. Use `map-ont` for nanopore, `map-pb` for PacBio.
4. `samtools mpileup -aa -f chimeric.fa realigned.bam` and feed it through the existing column-wise consensus logic in `breakpointPileup.pl`. Output is one FASTA record per fusion, indels at the junction reflected.

Main tradeoffs: (a) **window size matters more** — a 100bp/side chimeric ref is too small for minimap2 to reliably anchor reads, you probably want 300–500/side; (b) you're paying the cost of a second alignment pass per fusion, but it's small (only the supporting reads, against a tiny ref); (c) reads whose junction region is too noisy may fail to map and drop out of the consensus, which is actually a feature — they were going to be noise anyway.

Want me to sketch this as a concrete script (`reAlignFusion.sh` or similar) building on the existing tools, or should we first decide on the window size / aligner preset?

---

_**User (2026-04-30 03:58:53Z)**_

Is this an approach that has been taken or is this a novel approach

---

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-7 2026-04-30 03:59:11Z)**_

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-7 2026-04-30 03:59:19Z)**_

This is a well-established pattern, not novel. Variants of "build a chimeric/breakpoint reference, realign supporting reads to it, take a consensus" appear in most modern structural-variant and fusion callers — Manta and GRIDSS treat it as a core step (local assembly → realign), Pindel uses a split-read variant, and on the fusion side JAFFAL, LongGF, and Arriba all construct candidate junction sequences and validate by realigning. It's also the standard workflow when people want a clean breakpoint sequence for primer design or visualization.

What's potentially worth doing in this repo isn't the method itself but the **integration**: wiring it into the BFF output (cluster directory in, consensus FASTA out), keeping the variant-aware consensus convention you already have, and using minimap2's nanopore presets so it does the right thing for long reads. So the question to answer first isn't "should we invent this," it's "is the realignment-based consensus enough better than the current per-side mpileup to be worth the second alignment pass?" — most of the gain shows up when you have indels right at the junction or the read's junction region is heavily soft-clipped in the genome alignment.

If you want, I can pull a couple of those tools' breakpoint-consensus outputs and compare against what the current `breakpointPileup.pl` produces on your two test clusters before deciding to build it.

---

_**User (2026-04-30 04:01:49Z)**_

The reason for analysis is precisely to get a clean breakpoint sequence for primer design. If there is a pre-existing tool, I would prefer to use it rather than designing a new one unless I know the existing one has flaws or is difficult to apply to noisy long reads...

---

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-7 2026-04-30 04:02:22Z)**_

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-7 2026-04-30 04:02:37Z)**_

Honest take: for noisy long reads specifically, **pileup-against-reference is the wrong primitive for primer design**, and there are existing tools that do this much better. Your test clusters have 2 reads each — at that depth, mpileup-style consensus won't denoise nanopore's ~5-10% per-base error rate, and indels at the junction (the very thing you care about for primer mispriming) get smeared out because each read drops them in slightly different places.

What people actually use for clean long-read breakpoint sequences:

- **POA-based consensus** (`abPOA`, `spoa`, `lamassemble`) — partial-order alignment of the supporting reads to each other, then a consensus path through the graph. Handles noisy long reads much better than pileup because indels of the same event in different reads collapse into the same node. `lamassemble` specifically targets this use case and outputs a polished consensus FASTA.
- **Sniffles2** — long-read SV caller that emits consensus sequences for breakpoints natively (`--output-rnames` plus the per-SV consensus). If your data already has Sniffles2 output, the breakpoint sequence is sitting in the VCF.
- **JAFFAL** — long-read fusion-specific. Reports breakpoint sequences but they're not always primer-ready out of the box; you typically still need a polish.
- **Polishing on top** — once you have a draft (chimeric realignment, JAFFAL output, whatever), run `racon` and/or `medaka` (nanopore) over the supporting reads to clean it up. This is the standard final step for primer-grade sequences.

My recommended stack:

1. Use [extractReadsFasta.sh](bin/extractReadsFasta.sh) to pull the supporting reads for each cluster (we just built it — same files we need here).
2. Pipe them into `lamassemble` or `abPOA` per cluster to get a draft consensus that already handles indels properly.
3. (Optional) `medaka_consensus` polish if it's nanopore data.

That replaces `breakpointPileup.pl` entirely for the primer-design use case. The current pileup approach stays useful for *variant calling* in higher-depth fusion regions, but for "give me a clean junction sequence I can design primers against," POA + polish is the right answer.

The flaws to watch for: `lamassemble` and `abPOA` don't know about your reference, so the consensus is whatever the reads say — if the cluster has only 2 reads, you can still end up with a noisy answer. The reference-anchored realignment idea would help in that low-depth regime, but in practice you should probably just require a minimum cluster depth before designing primers from it.

Want me to wire `lamassemble` (or `abPOA`) into a small wrapper that takes a `breakpoint_files/`-style directory and a FASTQ, extracts per-cluster reads, and emits per-cluster polished consensus — so you can compare against the current pileup output on your two test clusters?

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-7 2026-04-30 04:05:04Z)**_

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-7 2026-04-30 04:05:16Z)**_

Good check, and worth correcting myself: **all of those are long-read tools, not Illumina**. `lamassemble`, `abPOA`, and `spoa` were written specifically for noisy long-read consensus (`lamassemble` by Frith et al. explicitly targets nanopore/PacBio); `sniffles2` is long-read-only; `racon` and `medaka` are the standard long-read polishers (medaka is ONT-specific). They were built precisely because Illumina-era tools choke on the indel rates you're worried about.

That said, **your concern about leaving the reference out is still legitimate at low cluster depth**. POA-only consensus handles indels well when it has enough redundancy across reads, but at 2-read clusters (your test case) random per-read indels can still leak through into the consensus because there's no second-opinion arbiter. The real answer is to use **both** — reference as the scaffold, reads as the polish:

1. **Reference-anchored draft** — build the chimeric ref from the genome FASTA the way I described. This pins the non-junction bases to ground truth and is why low-depth clusters don't fall apart.
2. **Realign the supporting reads** to it with minimap2 (`map-ont` for nanopore).
3. **Polish with `racon`** — feed in the chimeric ref + the realigned reads + the supporting FASTQ. Racon is purpose-built for the "draft + noisy long reads → clean consensus" loop, fixes indels per read coherently, and uses POA internally over the alignment.
4. (Optional) **`medaka_consensus`** on top — neural-network polisher trained on ONT data, squeezes out homopolymer-run errors specifically.

That stack gives you the reference's accuracy on the flanks (where you'll place primers) and read-supported edits at the junction (the part the reference can't tell you), without trusting POA alone at low depth.

Want me to wire `racon` (and optionally `medaka`) on top of the chimeric-realignment pipeline as a single wrapper, and add it to the BFF image so it ships alongside the existing scripts?

<tool-use data-tool-type="write" data-tool-name="Write"><details>

```sh
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# polishFusionConsensus.sh
#
# For each cluster in a BFF-style breakpoint_files/ directory, build a
# chimeric reference (5' partner window + 3' partner window in transcript
# order, RC where the breakpoint encoding says -), realign the supporting
# reads to it with minimap2 (map-ont preset), then polish with racon to
# produce a primer-design-grade consensus FASTA. Output is one record
# per cluster, named after the cluster.
#
# Why this exists: pileup-against-genome (processBreakpointDir.sh) is fast
# and gives a sensible per-side sequence at the breakpoint, but at low
# cluster depth on noisy long reads it doesn't denoise indels well, and
# bases right at the junction tend to be soft-clipped in the genome
# alignment. Realigning to a chimeric ref + racon polishing gives one
# clean record across the junction with indels handled coherently.
#
# Usage:
#   polishFusionConsensus.sh INPUT_DIR REFERENCE_FASTA OUTPUT_DIR [WINDOW]
#
# Defaults: WINDOW=300 (each side of the junction). Bigger than the
# pileup script's 100 because minimap2 needs more anchor to map noisy
# long reads to a small chimeric contig.
#
# Requires samtools, minimap2, racon, bash, awk on PATH. The
# biodepot/bff:test image bundles all of these.

set -euo pipefail

inDir=${1:-}
ref=${2:-}
outDir=${3:-}
window=${4:-300}

if [[ -z "$inDir" || -z "$ref" || -z "$outDir" ]]; then
    echo "usage: $0 INPUT_DIR REFERENCE_FASTA OUTPUT_DIR [WINDOW]" >&2
    exit 1
fi

[[ -d "$inDir" ]] || { echo "input dir not found: $inDir" >&2; exit 1; }
[[ -f "$ref"  ]]  || { echo "reference not found: $ref"   >&2; exit 1; }

if [[ ! -f "$ref.fai" ]]; then
    echo "indexing $ref (one-time)..." >&2
    samtools faidx "$ref"
fi

mkdir -p "$outDir"

# Detect contig naming: do we need to strip "chr" off the cluster SAMs to
# match the reference?
faiHasChr=0
firstFaiName=$(awk 'NR==1{print $1; exit}' "$ref.fai")
[[ "$firstFaiName" == chr* ]] && faiHasChr=1

# SAM header for sorting cluster alignments.
header=$(mktemp)
trap 'rm -f "$header"' EXIT
{
    printf '@HD\tVN:1.6\tSO:coordinate\n'
    awk -v OFS='\t' '{print "@SQ", "SN:" $1, "LN:" $2}' "$ref.fai"
} >"$header"

revcomp() {
    # stdin -> reverse complement on stdout. Preserves N case.
    tr 'ACGTacgtNn' 'TGCAtgcaNn' | rev
}

faExtract() {
    # samtools faidx, strip the header, join into one line.
    samtools faidx "$ref" "$1" | awk 'NR>1' | tr -d '\n'
}

shopt -s nullglob
processed=0
skipped=0
fellback=0
for samFile in "$inDir"/*.nearby.sam; do
    base=$(basename "$samFile" .nearby.sam)
    coordsFile="$inDir/${base}.nearby.readnamescoords"
    if [[ ! -f "$coordsFile" ]]; then
        echo "warn: missing $coordsFile, skipping $base" >&2
        skipped=$((skipped+1))
        continue
    fi

    # Parse canonical bp from filename: chrA-posA-chrB-posB
    if [[ ! "$base" =~ ^([^-]+)-([0-9]+)-([^-]+)-([0-9]+)$ ]]; then
        echo "warn: $base does not match chrA-posA-chrB-posB, skipping" >&2
        skipped=$((skipped+1))
        continue
    fi
    chrA="${BASH_REMATCH[1]}"; posA="${BASH_REMATCH[2]}"
    chrB="${BASH_REMATCH[3]}"; posB="${BASH_REMATCH[4]}"

    # Pull a representative encoding to recover strand. Prefer the row that
    # matches the filename's bp, fall back to the first row.
    encoding=$(awk -v FS='\t' -v pA="$posA" -v pB="$posB" '
        {
            n = split($2, sides, ";")
            if (n != 2) next
            split(sides[1], p1, ":")
            split(sides[2], p2, ":")
            if (p1[3] == pA && p2[2] == pB) { print $2; exit }
        }' "$coordsFile")
    [[ -z "$encoding" ]] && encoding=$(awk 'NR==1{print $2; exit}' "$coordsFile")

    side1Encoded="${encoding%%;*}"
    side2Encoded="${encoding#*;}"
    s1Start=$(echo "$side1Encoded" | cut -d: -f2)
    s1End=$(echo   "$side1Encoded" | cut -d: -f3)
    s2Start=$(echo "$side2Encoded" | cut -d: -f2)
    s2End=$(echo   "$side2Encoded" | cut -d: -f3)
    [[ "$s1Start" -gt "$s1End" ]] && dirA='-' || dirA='+'
    [[ "$s2Start" -gt "$s2End" ]] && dirB='-' || dirB='+'

    # Resolve contig names against the reference (strip chr if FAI is bare).
    refChrA="$chrA"; refChrB="$chrB"
    if [[ $faiHasChr -eq 0 ]]; then
        [[ "$chrA" == chr* ]] && refChrA="${chrA#chr}"
        [[ "$chrB" == chr* ]] && refChrB="${chrB#chr}"
    fi

    # Window placement (same logic as breakpointPileup.pl):
    #   5'+ or 3'-: window ends at bp going up genomically  -> [bp-W+1, bp]
    #   5'- or 3'+: window starts at bp going up genomically -> [bp, bp+W-1]
    if [[ $dirA == '+' ]]; then loA=$((posA - window + 1)); hiA=$posA
    else                        loA=$posA; hiA=$((posA + window - 1)); fi
    if [[ $dirB == '-' ]]; then loB=$((posB - window + 1)); hiB=$posB
    else                        loB=$posB; hiB=$((posB + window - 1)); fi
    [[ $loA -lt 1 ]] && loA=1
    [[ $loB -lt 1 ]] && loB=1

    work=$(mktemp -d)

    # 1. Build chimeric reference (5' + 3', in transcript order).
    sideA=$(faExtract "${refChrA}:${loA}-${hiA}")
    sideB=$(faExtract "${refChrB}:${loB}-${hiB}")
    [[ $dirA == '-' ]] && sideA=$(printf '%s' "$sideA" | revcomp)
    [[ $dirB == '-' ]] && sideB=$(printf '%s' "$sideB" | revcomp)
    chimericName="${chrA}_${posA}${dirA}_${chrB}_${posB}${dirB}"
    {
        printf '>%s\n' "$chimericName"
        printf '%s%s\n' "$sideA" "$sideB"
    } >"$work/chimeric.fa"
    samtools faidx "$work/chimeric.fa"

    # 2. Pull supporting reads' sequences out of the cluster SAM. The SAM
    # is headerless; prepend the FAI-derived header, sort, then samtools
    # fasta gives reads back in their original orientation.
    if [[ $faiHasChr -eq 0 ]]; then
        {
            cat "$header"
            awk -v FS='\t' -v OFS='\t' \
                '{ sub(/^chr/, "", $3); if ($7 != "*" && $7 != "=") sub(/^chr/, "", $7); print }' \
                "$samFile"
        }
    else
        cat "$header" "$samFile"
    fi | samtools sort -O bam -o "$work/cluster.bam" -
    samtools fasta "$work/cluster.bam" 2>/dev/null >"$work/reads.fa" || true
    if [[ ! -s "$work/reads.fa" ]]; then
        echo "warn: no reads extracted for $base, skipping" >&2
        rm -rf "$work"
        skipped=$((skipped+1))
        continue
    fi

    # 3. Realign reads to the chimeric ref. SAM (not BAM) for racon.
    minimap2 -ax map-ont --secondary=no \
        "$work/chimeric.fa" "$work/reads.fa" 2>"$work/minimap2.log" \
        >"$work/realign.sam"

    # 4. Polish. Racon can fail with very low coverage / no overlaps; if
    # it does, we fall back to the unpolished chimeric draft and flag it.
    if racon -t 1 "$work/reads.fa" "$work/realign.sam" "$work/chimeric.fa" \
            2>"$work/racon.log" >"$outDir/${base}.fa"; then
        echo "ok: $base -> $outDir/${base}.fa (polished)"
    else
        cp "$work/chimeric.fa" "$outDir/${base}.fa"
        echo "warn: racon failed for $base (likely low overlap); kept draft chimeric ref" >&2
        echo "      racon log: $(cat "$work/racon.log" | tr '\n' ' ' | head -c 200)" >&2
        fellback=$((fellback+1))
    fi

    rm -rf "$work"
    processed=$((processed+1))
done

echo "processed=$processed skipped=$skipped fallback_to_draft=$fellback output=$outDir"

```

```
ARG ALPINE_VERSION=3.15

FROM alpine:${ALPINE_VERSION} AS alpine-build-samtools

ARG SAMTOOLS_VERSION=1.13

RUN apk update && apk add build-base wget zlib-dev tar bzip2-dev xz-dev \
    curl-dev ncurses-dev ncurses-static curl-static zlib-static bzip2-static \
    nghttp2-static openssl-libs-static brotli-static

WORKDIR /build
RUN wget https://github.com/samtools/samtools/releases/download/${SAMTOOLS_VERSION}/samtools-${SAMTOOLS_VERSION}.tar.bz2
RUN tar -xf samtools-${SAMTOOLS_VERSION}.tar.bz2
WORKDIR /build/samtools-${SAMTOOLS_VERSION}
RUN ./configure && \
    make -j 4
RUN strip samtools

FROM alpine:${ALPINE_VERSION} AS alpine-build-minimap2
ARG MINIMAP2_VERSION=2.24
RUN apk add --no-cache build-base zlib-dev wget tar
WORKDIR /build
RUN wget -O minimap2.tar.bz2 https://github.com/lh3/minimap2/releases/download/v${MINIMAP2_VERSION}/minimap2-${MINIMAP2_VERSION}.tar.bz2
RUN tar -xjf minimap2.tar.bz2
WORKDIR /build/minimap2-${MINIMAP2_VERSION}
RUN make -j 4 && strip minimap2

FROM alpine:${ALPINE_VERSION} AS alpine-build-racon
ARG RACON_VERSION=1.5.0
RUN apk add --no-cache build-base zlib-dev cmake bash git linux-headers
WORKDIR /build
RUN git clone --recursive --branch ${RACON_VERSION} --depth 1 https://github.com/lbcb-sci/racon.git
WORKDIR /build/racon/build
RUN cmake -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Release .. && make -j 4 && strip bin/racon

FROM alpine:${ALPINE_VERSION}
ARG SAMTOOLS_VERSION=1.13
ARG MINIMAP2_VERSION=2.24
RUN apk add --no-cache bash perl libbz2 zlib libcurl xz-libs libstdc++ libgcc \
    && rm -rf /var/cache/apk/*
WORKDIR /
COPY --from=alpine-build-samtools /build/samtools-${SAMTOOLS_VERSION}/samtools /usr/local/bin/samtools
COPY --from=alpine-build-minimap2 /build/minimap2-${MINIMAP2_VERSION}/minimap2 /usr/local/bin/minimap2
COPY --from=alpine-build-racon    /build/racon/build/bin/racon                  /usr/local/bin/racon
ADD filterSam.pl /usr/local/bin/filterSam.pl
ADD filterEnds.pl /usr/local/bin/filterEnds.pl
ADD breakpointPileup.pl /usr/local/bin/breakpointPileup.pl
ADD processBreakpointDir.sh /usr/local/bin/processBreakpointDir.sh
ADD polishFusionConsensus.sh /usr/local/bin/polishFusionConsensus.sh
ADD runBFF.sh /usr/local/bin/runBFF.sh

```

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-7 2026-04-30 04:13:02Z)**_

Both 2-read clusters polished successfully. Comparing what each pipeline produces:

| Pipeline | Output per cluster | Length | Indels handled |
|----------|-------------------|--------|-----------------|
| `processBreakpointDir.sh` (mpileup) | 2 records × 100 bp | 200 bp total, split | No (column-wise only) |
| `polishFusionConsensus.sh` (racon) | 1 record (chimeric) | ~620 bp continuous | Yes (read-level) |

Racon's header reports `RC:i:2 XC:f:1.000000` — it processed both supporting reads and produced a contiguous junction sequence with indels resolved. The output is a single continuous consensus across the junction, which is what you actually want for primer design.

Image is 48.8 MB (only +2.3 MB over the previous size for both new tools). Files in this change:

- new: [bin/polishFusionConsensus.sh](bin/polishFusionConsensus.sh), Dockerfiles copy
- modified: [Dockerfile](widgets/fast_fusion_finder/BiodepotFusionFinder/Dockerfiles/Dockerfile) — adds two multi-stage builds (minimap2, racon) and the script ADD line

Two notes worth flagging:

- Racon discards the lowercase-variant convention `breakpointPileup.pl` uses — its output is plain uppercase. If you want a "variants in lowercase" pass on the polished sequence, that's a separate step (re-align polished to genome, mark mismatches). Easy to add but separate.
- 2-read clusters are racon's edge case; the output is technically valid but fundamentally limited by depth. The script still falls back to the unpolished chimeric draft if racon fails outright.

Want me to commit + push and update the README to document the new wrapper?

 `./README.md`

```text
1	# Fusion breakpoint consensus
2	
3	Build per-base consensus sequences over the supporting reads for every
4	fusion breakpoint detected by the BFF pipeline. The two halves of each
5	fusion are emitted as **separate FASTA records** (5' partner, then 3'
6	partner), windowed around the junction; columns where a non-reference
7	base wins a strict majority of the reads are emitted in lowercase.
8	
9	Two entry points:
10	
11	- [`bin/breakpointPileup.pl`](bin/breakpointPileup.pl) — the core script.
12	  Consumes a read-names list, the original BAM, the reference FASTA, and
13	  a breakpoint file; produces one FASTA record per side and (optionally)
14	  a per-position TSV of base counts. Use this when you already have
15	  those four pieces.
16	- [`bin/processBreakpointDir.sh`](bin/processBreakpointDir.sh) — batch
17	  wrapper for a `breakpoint_files/`-style directory. For each cluster it
18	  rebuilds the SAM into a sorted BAM, derives the reads list and the
19	  breakpoint encoding from `*.nearby.readnamescoords`, and runs the core
20	  script. Use this when you have BFF cluster output and a reference.
21	
22	## Layout
23	
24	```
25	bin/
26	├── breakpointPileup.pl       canonical script
27	├── processBreakpointDir.sh   batch wrapper
28	├── filterEnds.pl             upstream: read-name filter
29	└── ...
30	breakpointPileup.pl           symlink → bin/breakpointPileup.pl
```
</details></tool-use>

```text
33	## Layout
34	
35	```
36	bin/
37	├── breakpointPileup.pl       canonical script
38	├── processBreakpointDir.sh   batch wrapper
39	├── filterEnds.pl             upstream: read-name filter
40	└── ...
41	breakpointPileup.pl           symlink → bin/breakpointPileup.pl
42	tests/test_breakpointPileup.sh   self-contained fixture test
43	widgets/.../Dockerfiles/      copies that get baked into biodepot/bff:test
44	```
45	
46	`breakpointPileup.pl` lives at `bin/breakpointPileup.pl`; the top-level
47	file is a symlink for convenience. The Dockerfiles directory holds a
48	build-context copy of each script (kept in sync manually).
49	
50	## Requirements
51	
52	There is nothing to compile.
53	
54	- `samtools` 1.12 or newer on `$PATH` (1.12 added `samtools view -N`,
55	  the read-name filter the script depends on)
56	- `perl` 5 with `Getopt::Long` and `File::Temp` (both core)
57	- the reference FASTA must have a `.fai` (built lazily by the wrapper
58	  when missing, but only if the directory is writable)
59	
60	Verify:
61	
62	```bash
63	samtools --version | head -1   # 1.12+
64	perl -c bin/breakpointPileup.pl
65	```
66	
67	## Three ways to run
68	
69	### 1. Container (zero host setup)
70	
71	The `biodepot/bff:test` image bundles `samtools`, `perl`, and both
72	scripts under `/usr/local/bin`.
73	
74	```bash
75	# build once
76	docker build -t biodepot/bff:test \
77	    widgets/fast_fusion_finder/BiodepotFusionFinder/Dockerfiles
78	
79	# run on a breakpoint_files-style directory
80	docker run --rm \
81	    -u "$(id -u):$(id -g)" \
82	    -v /path/to/breakpoint_files:/in:ro \
83	    -v /path/to/reference_dir:/ref:ro \
84	    -v /path/to/output:/out \
85	    biodepot/bff:test \
86	    processBreakpointDir.sh /in /ref/genome.fa /out 100
87	```
88	
89	The `-u` flag makes the output files owned by your host user instead of
90	root. The reference directory needs the `.fa.fai`; create it once on
91	the host with `samtools faidx genome.fa` if it isn't there.
92	
93	### 2. Standalone
94	
95	If `samtools` and `perl` are on the host, just put the repo's `bin/`
96	on `$PATH` (so the wrapper can find `breakpointPileup.pl`) and run:
97	
98	```bash
99	PATH="$PWD/bin:$PATH" \
100	bin/processBreakpointDir.sh \
101	    /path/to/breakpoint_files \
102	    /path/to/genome.fa \
```
</details></tool-use>

130	automatically when both `$BAM` and `$REFERENCE` are set:

140

142	`runBFF.sh` runs `filterEnds.pl` alone, matching the legacy behaviour.
143	
144	## Inputs
145	
146	### For `processBreakpointDir.sh`
147	
148	| Path | Purpose |
149	|------|---------|
150	| `INPUT_DIR` | A `breakpoint_files/`-style directory with `*.nearby.sam` (alignment dump, no `@HD`/`@SQ`) and matching `*.nearby.readnamescoords`. |
151	| `REFERENCE_FASTA` | The genome FASTA used for alignment. Needs a `.fai`. |
152	| `OUTPUT_DIR` | Created if missing. One `<name>.fa` and one `<name>.tsv` per cluster. |
153	| `WINDOW` | Optional half-window size in bp (default `100`). Each side gets `WINDOW` bases on the transcript-internal flank of the breakpoint. |
154	
155	The wrapper auto-detects UCSC vs Ensembl contig naming. If the SAM uses
156	`chr15`-style names but the reference doesn't, `chr` is stripped on the
157	fly from `RNAME`, `RNEXT`, and the breakpoint encoding before mpileup.
158	
159	### For `breakpointPileup.pl`
160	
161	| Flag | Required | Notes |
162	|------|----------|-------|
163	| `--reads reads.txt` | yes | Newline-separated read names — typically the stdout of `filterEnds.pl`. |
164	| `--bam in.bam` | yes | Coordinate-sorted and indexed. |

git -C /home/lhhung/fast_fusion_finder add bin/polishFusionConsensus.sh widgets/fast_fusion_finder/BiodepotFusionFinder/Dockerfiles/polishFusionConsensus.sh widgets/fast_fusion_finder/BiodepotFusionFinder/Dockerfiles/Dockerfile README.md .specstory/ && git -C /home/lhhung/fast_fusion_finder commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
Add polishFusionConsensus.sh: chimeric realign + racon polish

For primer-design-grade junction sequences, an mpileup against the
genome is the wrong primitive on noisy long reads — pileup-style
consensus doesn't denoise indels well at low depth, and bases right at
the junction tend to be soft-clipped in the genome alignment.

bin/polishFusionConsensus.sh
- Per cluster in a breakpoint_files/-style directory:
    1. extract a chimeric reference (5' window + 3' window in transcript
       order, RC where the breakpoint encoding says -) from the genome
       FASTA via samtools faidx;
    2. pull supporting reads' sequences from the cluster SAM with
       samtools fasta (FLAG-aware, in their original orientation);
    3. realign them to the chimeric ref with minimap2 -ax map-ont
       --secondary=no;
    4. polish with racon, falling back to the unpolished draft when
       racon refuses (zero-overlap edge cases).
- Default WINDOW=300 (vs 100 for the pileup wrapper) because minimap2
  needs a longer chimeric contig to anchor noisy reads.
- Same chr-prefix auto-detection and filename-canonical bp lookup as
  processBreakpointDir.sh.

Dockerfile
- Multi-stage builds for minimap2 2.24 and racon 1.5.0 alongside the
  existing samtools build. Final image grows from 46.5 MB to ~48.8 MB.
- libstdc++/libgcc added to the runtime layer for racon (C++).
- ADD line for the new wrapper.

Smoke-tested on the existing 2-read clusters in
/mnt/pikachu/bff-test/breakpoint_files: both clusters polish cleanly
producing one ~620-bp record per cluster (RC:i:2 XC:f:1.0). At 2 reads
the polish is depth-limited but the pipeline runs end-to-end.

README updated to document the polish wrapper, its use case (primer
design), the racon edge cases, and the lowercase-variant tradeoff
(racon emits plain uppercase; use the mpileup pipeline when you need
variant annotation).
EOF
)" && git -C /home/lhhung/fast_fusion_finder push 2>&1

<tool-use data-tool-type="write" data-tool-name="Write"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Write**</summary>

```sh
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# runFusionConsensus.sh
#
# Host-side driver. Builds biodepot/bff:test if it isn't already present,
# then runs either the polish (default) or the per-side pileup pipeline
# inside the container, and emits a TSV table of consensus sequences to
# stdout (or a file via -o).
#
# Two input modes:
#   -d INPUT_DIR        process every *.nearby.sam in INPUT_DIR
#   SAM [SAM ...]       process the given *.nearby.sam files explicitly
#                       (each must have its matching *.nearby.readnamescoords
#                        file alongside it)
#
# Output table (tab-separated):
#   cluster    side     length    sequence
# - polish mode: side = "junction", one row per cluster (chimeric +
#   racon, primer-design grade).
# - pileup mode: side = "5p" or "3p", two rows per cluster
#   (per-side mpileup consensus, lowercase variants preserved).
#
# Usage:
#   runFusionConsensus.sh [opts] -d INPUT_DIR REFERENCE.fa
#   runFusionConsensus.sh [opts]    REFERENCE.fa SAM [SAM ...]
#
# Options:
#   -m polish|pileup   pipeline (default: polish)
#   -w N               window size (default 300 polish / 100 pileup)
#   -o PATH            write TSV to PATH instead of stdout
#   -D DIR             keep the per-cluster FASTAs (and pileup TSVs) in
#                      DIR after the run; DIR is created if missing.
#                      Default: per-cluster artefacts are dropped.
#   -i IMAGE           override the docker image tag (default biodepot/bff:test)
#   -h                 this help

set -euo pipefail

MODE=polish
WINDOW=
OUT=-
SEARCH_DIR=
KEEP_DIR=
IMAGE=biodepot/bff:test

SCRIPT_DIR=$(cd "$(dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}")" && pwd)
DOCKERFILES_DIR="$SCRIPT_DIR/../widgets/fast_fusion_finder/BiodepotFusionFinder/Dockerfiles"

usage() {
    sed -n '/^# Usage:/,/^# Options:/p; /^#   -[A-Za-z]/p' "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}" \
        | sed 's/^# \{0,2\}//' >&2
    exit "${1:-1}"
}

while getopts "m:w:o:d:D:i:h" opt; do
    case "$opt" in
        m) MODE=$OPTARG;;
        w) WINDOW=$OPTARG;;
        o) OUT=$OPTARG;;
        d) SEARCH_DIR=$OPTARG;;
        D) KEEP_DIR=$OPTARG;;
        i) IMAGE=$OPTARG;;
        h) usage 0;;
        *) usage;;
    esac
done
shift $((OPTIND - 1))

case "$MODE" in
    polish) WRAPPER=polishFusionConsensus.sh; WINDOW=${WINDOW:-300};;
    pileup) WRAPPER=processBreakpointDir.sh;  WINDOW=${WINDOW:-100};;
    *) echo "unknown mode: $MODE (use polish or pileup)" >&2; exit 1;;
esac

command -v docker >/dev/null 2>&1 \
    || { echo "docker not found on PATH" >&2; exit 1; }

# Build the image if missing (and possible).
if ! docker image inspect "$IMAGE" >/dev/null 2>&1; then
    if [[ -d "$DOCKERFILES_DIR" ]]; then
        echo "image $IMAGE not found, building from $DOCKERFILES_DIR ..." >&2
        docker build -t "$IMAGE" "$DOCKERFILES_DIR" >&2
    else
        echo "image $IMAGE not found and Dockerfile dir not at $DOCKERFILES_DIR" >&2
        echo "build the image first:" >&2
        echo "  docker build -t $IMAGE <path-to-Dockerfiles>" >&2
        exit 1
    fi
fi

# Cleanup tracking
cleanup_dirs=()
cleanup() {
    local d
    for d in "${cleanup_dirs[@]+"${cleanup_dirs[@]}"}"; do
        [[ -n "$d" && -d "$d" ]] && rm -rf "$d"
    done
}
trap cleanup EXIT

# Resolve input source.
if [[ -n "$SEARCH_DIR" ]]; then
    [[ $# -lt 1 ]] && usage
    REF=$1; shift
    [[ $# -gt 0 ]] && { echo "extra args after reference: $*" >&2; usage; }
    [[ -d "$SEARCH_DIR" ]] || { echo "directory not found: $SEARCH_DIR" >&2; exit 1; }
    INPUT_DIR=$(realpath "$SEARCH_DIR")
else
    [[ $# -lt 2 ]] && usage
    REF=$1; shift
    INPUT_DIR=$(mktemp -d)
    cleanup_dirs+=("$INPUT_DIR")
    for sam in "$@"; do
        [[ -f "$sam" ]] || { echo "sam not found: $sam" >&2; exit 1; }
        sam=$(realpath "$sam")
        base=$(basename "$sam" .nearby.sam)
        if [[ "$(basename "$sam")" == "$base" ]]; then
            echo "expected *.nearby.sam, got: $sam" >&2; exit 1
        fi
        coords="$(dirname "$sam")/${base}.nearby.readnamescoords"
        [[ -f "$coords" ]] \
            || { echo "missing $coords (expected next to $sam)" >&2; exit 1; }
        # Hardlink first (fast, same FS); fall back to copy across FSes.
        ln "$sam"    "$INPUT_DIR/${base}.nearby.sam"               2>/dev/null \
            || cp "$sam"    "$INPUT_DIR/${base}.nearby.sam"
        ln "$coords" "$INPUT_DIR/${base}.nearby.readnamescoords"   2>/dev/null \
            || cp "$coords" "$INPUT_DIR/${base}.nearby.readnamescoords"
    done
fi
[[ -f "$REF" ]] || { echo "reference not found: $REF" >&2; exit 1; }
REF=$(realpath "$REF")
REF_DIR=$(dirname "$REF")
REF_NAME=$(basename "$REF")

# Per-cluster output dir (kept on -D, otherwise scratch).
if [[ -n "$KEEP_DIR" ]]; then
    mkdir -p "$KEEP_DIR"
    OUT_DIR=$(realpath "$KEEP_DIR")
else
    OUT_DIR=$(mktemp -d)
    cleanup_dirs+=("$OUT_DIR")
fi

# Run inside the container. All wrapper progress lines go to stderr so
# stdout stays clean for the table.
docker run --rm \
    -u "$(id -u):$(id -g)" \
    -v "$INPUT_DIR:/in:ro" \
    -v "$REF_DIR:/ref:ro" \
    -v "$OUT_DIR:/out" \
    "$IMAGE" \
    "$WRAPPER" /in "/ref/$REF_NAME" /out "$WINDOW" >&2

# Emit the TSV table.
[[ "$OUT" == "-" ]] && OUT=/dev/stdout
{
    printf 'cluster\tside\tlength\tsequence\n'
    shopt -s nullglob
    for fa in "$OUT_DIR"/*.fa; do
        base=$(basename "$fa" .fa)
        awk -v cluster="$base" -v mode="$MODE" '
            BEGIN { rec = 0; seq = ""; have = 0 }
            function emit() {
                side = (mode == "polish") ? "junction" : (rec == 1 ? "5p" : "3p")
                printf "%s\t%s\t%d\t%s\n", cluster, side, length(seq), seq
            }
            /^>/ {
                if (have) emit()
                have = 1; rec++; seq = ""
                next
            }
            { seq = seq $0 }
            END { if (have) emit() }
        ' "$fa"
    done
} > "$OUT"

```

1	#!/usr/bin/env bash
2	# runFusionConsensus.sh
3	#
4	# Host-side driver. Builds biodepot/bff:test if it isn't already present,
5	# then runs either the polish (default) or the per-side pileup pipeline
6	# inside the container, and emits a TSV table of consensus sequences to
7	# stdout (or a file via -o).
8	#
9	# Two input modes:
10	#   -d INPUT_DIR        process every *.nearby.sam in INPUT_DIR
11	#   SAM [SAM ...]       process the given *.nearby.sam files explicitly
12	#                       (each must have its matching *.nearby.readnamescoords
13	#                        file alongside it)
14	#
15	# Output table (tab-separated):
16	#   cluster    side     length    sequence
17	# - polish mode: side = "junction", one row per cluster (chimeric +
18	#   racon, primer-design grade).
19	# - pileup mode: side = "5p" or "3p", two rows per cluster
20	#   (per-side mpileup consensus, lowercase variants preserved).
21	#
22	# Usage:
23	#   runFusionConsensus.sh [opts] -d INPUT_DIR REFERENCE.fa
24	#   runFusionConsensus.sh [opts]    REFERENCE.fa SAM [SAM ...]
25	#
26	# Options:
27	#   -m polish|pileup   pipeline (default: polish)
28	#   -w N               window size (default 300 polish / 100 pileup)
29	#   -o PATH            write TSV to PATH instead of stdout
30	#   -D DIR             keep the per-cluster FASTAs (and pileup TSVs) in
31	#                      DIR after the run; DIR is created if missing.
32	#                      Default: per-cluster artefacts are dropped.
33	#   -i IMAGE           override the docker image tag (default biodepot/bff:test)
34	#   -h                 this help
35	
36	set -euo pipefail
37	
38	MODE=polish
39	WINDOW=
40	OUT=-
41	SEARCH_DIR=
42	KEEP_DIR=
43	IMAGE=biodepot/bff:test
44	
45	SCRIPT_DIR=$(cd "$(dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}")" && pwd)
46	DOCKERFILES_DIR="$SCRIPT_DIR/../widgets/fast_fusion_finder/BiodepotFusionFinder/Dockerfiles"
47	
48	usage() {
49	    cat <<'EOF' >&2
50	Usage:
51	  runFusionConsensus.sh [opts] -d INPUT_DIR REFERENCE.fa
52	  runFusionConsensus.sh [opts]    REFERENCE.fa SAM [SAM ...]
53	
54	Options:
55	  -m polish|pileup   pipeline (default: polish)
56	  -w N               window size (default 300 polish / 100 pileup)
57	  -o PATH            write TSV to PATH instead of stdout
58	  -D DIR             keep per-cluster FASTAs (and pileup TSVs) in DIR
59	  -d DIR             use every *.nearby.sam in DIR
60	  -i IMAGE           docker image tag (default biodepot/bff:test)
61	  -h                 show this help
62	EOF
63	    exit "${1:-1}"
64	}
65	
66	while getopts "m:w:o:d:D:i:h" opt; do
67	    case "$opt" in
68	        m) MODE=$OPTARG;;
69	        w) WINDOW=$OPTARG;;
70	        o) OUT=$OPTARG;;
71	        d) SEARCH_DIR=$OPTARG;;
72	        D) KEEP_DIR=$OPTARG;;
73	        i) IMAGE=$OPTARG;;
74	        h) usage 0;;
75	        *) usage;;
76	    esac
77	done
78	shift $((OPTIND - 1))
79	
80	case "$MODE" in
81	    polish) WRAPPER=polishFusionConsensus.sh; WINDOW=${WINDOW:-300};;
82	    pileup) WRAPPER=processBreakpointDir.sh;  WINDOW=${WINDOW:-100};;
83	    *) echo "unknown mode: $MODE (use polish or pileup)" >&2; exit 1;;
84	esac
85	
86	command -v docker >/dev/null 2>&1 \
87	    || { echo "docker not found on PATH" >&2; exit 1; }
88	
89	# Build the image if missing (and possible).
90	if ! docker image inspect "$IMAGE" >/dev/null 2>&1; then
91	    if [[ -d "$DOCKERFILES_DIR" ]]; then
92	        echo "image $IMAGE not found, building from $DOCKERFILES_DIR ..." >&2
93	        docker build -t "$IMAGE" "$DOCKERFILES_DIR" >&2
94	    else
95	        echo "image $IMAGE not found and Dockerfile dir not at $DOCKERFILES_DIR" >&2
96	        echo "build the image first:" >&2
97	        echo "  docker build -t $IMAGE <path-to-Dockerfiles>" >&2
98	        exit 1
99	    fi
100	fi
101	
102	# Cleanup tracking
103	cleanup_dirs=()
104	cleanup() {
105	    local d
106	    for d in "${cleanup_dirs[@]+"${cleanup_dirs[@]}"}"; do
107	        [[ -n "$d" && -d "$d" ]] && rm -rf "$d"
108	    done
109	}
110	trap cleanup EXIT
111	
112	# Resolve input source.
113	if [[ -n "$SEARCH_DIR" ]]; then
114	    [[ $# -lt 1 ]] && usage
115	    REF=$1; shift
116	    [[ $# -gt 0 ]] && { echo "extra args after reference: $*" >&2; usage; }
117	    [[ -d "$SEARCH_DIR" ]] || { echo "directory not found: $SEARCH_DIR" >&2; exit 1; }
118	    INPUT_DIR=$(realpath "$SEARCH_DIR")
119	else
120	    [[ $# -lt 2 ]] && usage
121	    REF=$1; shift
122	    INPUT_DIR=$(mktemp -d)
123	    cleanup_dirs+=("$INPUT_DIR")
124	    for sam in "$@"; do
125	        [[ -f "$sam" ]] || { echo "sam not found: $sam" >&2; exit 1; }
126	        sam=$(realpath "$sam")
127	        base=$(basename "$sam" .nearby.sam)
128	        if [[ "$(basename "$sam")" == "$base" ]]; then
129	            echo "expected *.nearby.sam, got: $sam" >&2; exit 1
130	        fi
131	        coords="$(dirname "$sam")/${base}.nearby.readnamescoords"
132	        [[ -f "$coords" ]] \
133	            || { echo "missing $coords (expected next to $sam)" >&2; exit 1; }
134	        # Hardlink first (fast, same FS); fall back to copy across FSes.
135	        ln "$sam"    "$INPUT_DIR/${base}.nearby.sam"               2>/dev/null \
136	            || cp "$sam"    "$INPUT_DIR/${base}.nearby.sam"
137	        ln "$coords" "$INPUT_DIR/${base}.nearby.readnamescoords"   2>/dev/null \
138	            || cp "$coords" "$INPUT_DIR/${base}.nearby.readnamescoords"
139	    done
140	fi
141	[[ -f "$REF" ]] || { echo "reference not found: $REF" >&2; exit 1; }
142	REF=$(realpath "$REF")
143	REF_DIR=$(dirname "$REF")
144	REF_NAME=$(basename "$REF")
145	
146	# Per-cluster output dir (kept on -D, otherwise scratch).
147	if [[ -n "$KEEP_DIR" ]]; then
148	    mkdir -p "$KEEP_DIR"
149	    OUT_DIR=$(realpath "$KEEP_DIR")
150	else
151	    OUT_DIR=$(mktemp -d)
152	    cleanup_dirs+=("$OUT_DIR")
153	fi
154	
155	# Run inside the container. All wrapper progress lines go to stderr so
156	# stdout stays clean for the table.
157	docker run --rm \
158	    -u "$(id -u):$(id -g)" \
159	    -v "$INPUT_DIR:/in:ro" \
160	    -v "$REF_DIR:/ref:ro" \
161	    -v "$OUT_DIR:/out" \
162	    "$IMAGE" \
163	    "$WRAPPER" /in "/ref/$REF_NAME" /out "$WINDOW" >&2
164	
165	# Emit the TSV table.
166	[[ "$OUT" == "-" ]] && OUT=/dev/stdout
167	{
168	    printf 'cluster\tside\tlength\tsequence\n'
169	    shopt -s nullglob
170	    for fa in "$OUT_DIR"/*.fa; do
171	        base=$(basename "$fa" .fa)
172	        awk -v cluster="$base" -v mode="$MODE" '
173	            BEGIN { rec = 0; seq = ""; have = 0 }
174	            function emit() {
175	                side = (mode == "polish") ? "junction" : (rec == 1 ? "5p" : "3p")
176	                printf "%s\t%s\t%d\t%s\n", cluster, side, length(seq), seq
177	            }
178	            /^>/ {
179	                if (have) emit()
180	                have = 1; rec++; seq = ""
181	                next
182	            }
183	            { seq = seq $0 }
184	            END { if (have) emit() }
185	        ' "$fa"
186	    done
187	} > "$OUT"
188
```

<tool-use data-tool-type="write" data-tool-name="Write"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Write**</summary>

```sh
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# runFusionConsensus.sh
#
# Top-level host-side driver for the BFF fusion-consensus pipeline.
#
# What it does:
#   1. Makes sure the biodepot/bff:test Docker image exists. If it
#      doesn't, builds it from the in-repo Dockerfiles directory.
#   2. Stages the input *.nearby.sam files (and matching
#      *.nearby.readnamescoords) into a directory the container can see.
#   3. Runs either the polish pipeline (chimeric-ref + minimap2 + racon
#      → primer-grade junction sequence) or the per-side mpileup
#      pipeline (variant-aware, two halves per cluster) inside the
#      container.
#   4. Walks the per-cluster FASTAs the container produced and prints a
#      tab-separated table to stdout (or to -o PATH).
#
# Two input modes:
#   -d INPUT_DIR        process every *.nearby.sam in INPUT_DIR
#   SAM [SAM ...]       process the given *.nearby.sam files explicitly
#                       (each must have its matching *.nearby.readnamescoords
#                        file alongside it)
#
# Output table (tab-separated):
#   cluster    side     length    sequence
# - polish mode: side = "junction", one row per cluster.
# - pileup mode: side = "5p" or "3p", two rows per cluster
#   (lowercase letters mark non-reference consensus calls).
#
# Run with --help (or -h) for the usage block plus an example.

set -euo pipefail

# ---- defaults --------------------------------------------------------------

MODE=polish                      # which container script to run
WINDOW=                          # half-window in bp; mode-specific default below
OUT=-                            # "-" means stdout; otherwise a file path
SEARCH_DIR=                      # set by -d; overrides positional SAM list
KEEP_DIR=                        # set by -D; preserves per-cluster FASTAs
IMAGE=biodepot/bff:test          # container tag; -i overrides

# Locate the in-repo Dockerfiles dir relative to this script. Used only
# if the image is missing and we need to build it. If the script is
# moved out of the repo (eg /usr/local/bin), this path won't resolve and
# the script will refuse to auto-build (telling the user how instead).
SCRIPT_DIR=$(cd "$(dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}")" && pwd)
DOCKERFILES_DIR="$SCRIPT_DIR/../widgets/fast_fusion_finder/BiodepotFusionFinder/Dockerfiles"

# ---- help ------------------------------------------------------------------

usage() {
    # Always written to stderr so the TSV stdout stays clean if someone
    # accidentally pipes runFusionConsensus.sh -h | column -t.
    cat <<'EOF' >&2
Usage:
  runFusionConsensus.sh [opts] -d INPUT_DIR REFERENCE.fa
  runFusionConsensus.sh [opts]    REFERENCE.fa SAM [SAM ...]

Options:
  -m polish|pileup   pipeline (default: polish)
                       polish: chimeric-ref + minimap2 + racon
                               -> one primer-grade record per cluster
                       pileup: per-side mpileup vs the genome
                               -> two records (5'/3') per cluster,
                                  lowercase letters = non-reference calls
  -w N               half-window size in bp
                       defaults: 300 (polish) / 100 (pileup)
                       polish needs more anchor for minimap2 to map
                       noisy long reads onto the small chimeric ref.
  -o PATH            write the TSV table to PATH instead of stdout
  -D DIR             keep the per-cluster FASTAs (and pileup .tsv files)
                     in DIR after the run; DIR is created if missing.
                     Default: per-cluster artefacts are dropped, only
                     the table survives.
  -d DIR             process every *.nearby.sam under DIR.
                     Mutually exclusive with passing SAM files
                     positionally.
  -i IMAGE           override the docker image tag
                     (default: biodepot/bff:test)
  -h, --help         show this help and exit

Output (TSV, on stdout unless -o):
  cluster<TAB>side<TAB>length<TAB>sequence
    polish: side = "junction", one row per cluster
    pileup: side = "5p" / "3p", two rows per cluster

Example:
  # Polish every cluster in a breakpoint_files/ dir using the
  # cellranger Ensembl reference; redirect the table to a file.
  bin/runFusionConsensus.sh \
      -d /mnt/pikachu/bff-test/breakpoint_files \
      -o consensus.tsv \
      /mnt/pikachu/autoindex_110_44/cellranger_ref_cache/Homo_sapiens.GRCh38.dna.primary_assembly.fa

Notes:
  * Only docker is required on the host; the image carries samtools,
    perl, minimap2, racon, and the wrapper scripts.
  * The reference FASTA must have a .fai (the in-container wrappers
    will create one lazily if its directory is writable).
  * The SAM files passed positionally must end in .nearby.sam and have
    their .nearby.readnamescoords companion next to them.
EOF
    exit "${1:-1}"
}

# Translate --help into -h before getopts sees it. getopts only handles
# single-char short options.
for arg in "$@"; do
    [[ "$arg" == "--help" ]] && usage 0
done

# ---- option parsing --------------------------------------------------------

while getopts "m:w:o:d:D:i:h" opt; do
    case "$opt" in
        m) MODE=$OPTARG;;        # polish | pileup
        w) WINDOW=$OPTARG;;      # half-window override
        o) OUT=$OPTARG;;         # TSV destination
        d) SEARCH_DIR=$OPTARG;;  # directory-mode input
        D) KEEP_DIR=$OPTARG;;    # preserve per-cluster artefacts
        i) IMAGE=$OPTARG;;       # custom image tag
        h) usage 0;;
        *) usage;;
    esac
done
shift $((OPTIND - 1))

# Pick the in-container wrapper script and apply mode-specific window.
case "$MODE" in
    polish) WRAPPER=polishFusionConsensus.sh; WINDOW=${WINDOW:-300};;
    pileup) WRAPPER=processBreakpointDir.sh;  WINDOW=${WINDOW:-100};;
    *) echo "unknown mode: $MODE (use polish or pileup)" >&2; exit 1;;
esac

# ---- preflight: docker + image --------------------------------------------

command -v docker >/dev/null 2>&1 \
    || { echo "docker not found on PATH" >&2; exit 1; }

# Build the image if missing AND we know where the Dockerfiles live.
if ! docker image inspect "$IMAGE" >/dev/null 2>&1; then
    if [[ -d "$DOCKERFILES_DIR" ]]; then
        echo "image $IMAGE not found, building from $DOCKERFILES_DIR ..." >&2
        docker build -t "$IMAGE" "$DOCKERFILES_DIR" >&2
    else
        echo "image $IMAGE not found and Dockerfile dir not at $DOCKERFILES_DIR" >&2
        echo "build the image first:" >&2
        echo "  docker build -t $IMAGE <path-to-Dockerfiles>" >&2
        exit 1
    fi
fi

# ---- cleanup tracking ------------------------------------------------------

# We may create up to two tempdirs (an input staging dir for explicit
# SAM-file mode, and an output dir if -D wasn't given). Track them so
# the EXIT trap can clean them up after the table has been emitted.
cleanup_dirs=()
cleanup() {
    local d
    for d in "${cleanup_dirs[@]+"${cleanup_dirs[@]}"}"; do
        [[ -n "$d" && -d "$d" ]] && rm -rf "$d"
    done
}
trap cleanup EXIT

# ---- resolve inputs --------------------------------------------------------

if [[ -n "$SEARCH_DIR" ]]; then
    # Directory mode: -d INPUT_DIR REFERENCE.fa
    [[ $# -lt 1 ]] && usage
    REF=$1; shift
    [[ $# -gt 0 ]] && { echo "extra args after reference: $*" >&2; usage; }
    [[ -d "$SEARCH_DIR" ]] || { echo "directory not found: $SEARCH_DIR" >&2; exit 1; }
    INPUT_DIR=$(realpath "$SEARCH_DIR")
else
    # Explicit SAM mode: REFERENCE.fa SAM [SAM ...]
    [[ $# -lt 2 ]] && usage
    REF=$1; shift

    # Stage the SAM/coords pair for each cluster into a tempdir so the
    # in-container wrapper sees a well-shaped directory. Hardlink first
    # (no copy when on the same FS), fall back to cp across filesystems.
    INPUT_DIR=$(mktemp -d)
    cleanup_dirs+=("$INPUT_DIR")
    for sam in "$@"; do
        [[ -f "$sam" ]] || { echo "sam not found: $sam" >&2; exit 1; }
        sam=$(realpath "$sam")
        base=$(basename "$sam" .nearby.sam)
        if [[ "$(basename "$sam")" == "$base" ]]; then
            echo "expected *.nearby.sam, got: $sam" >&2; exit 1
        fi
        coords="$(dirname "$sam")/${base}.nearby.readnamescoords"
        [[ -f "$coords" ]] \
            || { echo "missing $coords (expected next to $sam)" >&2; exit 1; }
        ln "$sam"    "$INPUT_DIR/${base}.nearby.sam"             2>/dev/null \
            || cp "$sam"    "$INPUT_DIR/${base}.nearby.sam"
        ln "$coords" "$INPUT_DIR/${base}.nearby.readnamescoords" 2>/dev/null \
            || cp "$coords" "$INPUT_DIR/${base}.nearby.readnamescoords"
    done
fi

# Reference: must exist; resolve to absolute path so docker -v sees it.
[[ -f "$REF" ]] || { echo "reference not found: $REF" >&2; exit 1; }
REF=$(realpath "$REF")
REF_DIR=$(dirname "$REF")
REF_NAME=$(basename "$REF")

# ---- per-cluster artefact dir ---------------------------------------------

# OUT_DIR holds the .fa (and, in pileup mode, .tsv) files the wrapper
# emits. With -D we keep them; otherwise we make a tempdir that the
# EXIT trap removes after we've built the table from them.
if [[ -n "$KEEP_DIR" ]]; then
    mkdir -p "$KEEP_DIR"
    OUT_DIR=$(realpath "$KEEP_DIR")
else
    OUT_DIR=$(mktemp -d)
    cleanup_dirs+=("$OUT_DIR")
fi

# ---- run the in-container pipeline ----------------------------------------

# All wrapper progress goes to stderr so stdout is reserved for the TSV.
# -u keeps the host user as the owner of files written into OUT_DIR.
docker run --rm \
    -u "$(id -u):$(id -g)" \
    -v "$INPUT_DIR:/in:ro" \
    -v "$REF_DIR:/ref:ro" \
    -v "$OUT_DIR:/out" \
    "$IMAGE" \
    "$WRAPPER" /in "/ref/$REF_NAME" /out "$WINDOW" >&2

# ---- build and emit the TSV table -----------------------------------------

# "-" -> /dev/stdout so the same { ... } > "$OUT" works either way.
[[ "$OUT" == "-" ]] && OUT=/dev/stdout

{
    printf 'cluster\tside\tlength\tsequence\n'

    # Iterate over each cluster's FASTA. Each file has 1 record (polish)
    # or 2 records (pileup, 5'/3'). awk concatenates the wrapped sequence
    # lines into a single string and emits one TSV row per record.
    shopt -s nullglob
    for fa in "$OUT_DIR"/*.fa; do
        base=$(basename "$fa" .fa)
        awk -v cluster="$base" -v mode="$MODE" '
            BEGIN { rec = 0; seq = ""; have = 0 }

            # Emit the most-recently-collected record (called when we
            # see the next ">" or hit EOF).
            function emit() {
                side = (mode == "polish") ? "junction" : (rec == 1 ? "5p" : "3p")
                printf "%s\t%s\t%d\t%s\n", cluster, side, length(seq), seq
            }

            /^>/ {
                if (have) emit()
                have = 1; rec++; seq = ""
                next
            }

            # All non-header lines are sequence; concatenate.
            { seq = seq $0 }

            END { if (have) emit() }
        ' "$fa"
    done
} > "$OUT"

```
